result.markdown:
--- title: "Data Wrangling II" format: revealjs: theme: [simple, style.scss] auto-stretch: false editor: source --- ## DATE Today we will... - New Material - Extend `dplyr` verbs to have more functionality. - Data pivots. - PA 7: Military Spending # Extending `dplyr` verbs ## Reminder: Example Data [Cereal](https://search.r-project.org/CRAN/refmans/liver/html/cereal.html) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(liver) data(cereal) head(cereal, n = 5) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## More `dplyr` - We have already covered a lot, but not everything you might want... - Today we will cover functions that help with the following tasks: - extract a variable as a vector - simple frequency table of a categorical variable - creating a categorical variable from levels of a quantitatie variable - applying slice to groups and multiple variables - mutating or summarizing many variables at once ## `pull()` What is the mean potassium for cold cereals? 🧩 . . . - You can't use the `$` operator in a pipeline - `pull()` to the rescue! - `pull()` extracts a data frame column as a *vector* . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> filter(type == "cold") |> pull(potass) |> mean(na.rm = T) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## Reminder: count with `summarize()` How many cereals does each `manuf` have in this dataset? . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> group_by(manuf) |> summarize(n = n()) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## Count with `count()` How many cereals does each `manuf` have in this dataset? . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> count(manuf) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## `if_else()` For each cereal, label the `potass` as "high" or "low". . . . One `if-else` statement: - `if_else(, , )` . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> mutate(po_category = if_else(potass <= 100, "low", "high"), .after = potass) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: `.after` -- specifies the location of the newly created column ## `case_when()` For each cereal, label the amount of sugar as "low", "medium", "high", or "very high". . . . A **series** of `if-else` statements. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> mutate(sugar_level = case_when(sugars == -1 ~ NA_character_, sugars < 2 ~ "low", sugars < 5 ~ "medium", sugars < 10 ~ "high", .default = "very high")) |> select(name, sugars, sugar_level) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## Summarize multiple columns For each `type` of cereal, calculate the `mean` nutrient levels. . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> group_by(type) |> summarize(mean_calories = mean(calories, na.rm = T), mean_protein = mean(protein, na.rm = T), mean_fat = mean(fat, na.rm = T), mean_sodium = mean(sodium, na.rm = T), . . ., mean_vitamins = mean(vitamins, na.rm = T)) ``` ::: . . . :::callout-warning # SO MUCH COPY-PASTE! There are 9 different nutrient columns in the dataset! There has to be a better way... ::: ## Summarize multiple columns with `across()` For each `type` of cereal, calculate the `mean` nutrient levels. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> group_by(type) |> summarise(across(.cols = calories:potass, .fns = \(x) mean(x, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: :::callout-tip # So much better! ::: ## Summarize multiple columns with `across()` **Within** the `summarize()` function, we use the `across()` function, with two arguments: - `.cols` -- to specify the **columns** to apply functions to. - `.fns` -- to specify the **functions** to apply. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> group_by(type) |> summarise(across(.cols = calories:potass, .fns = \(x) mean(x, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: Use **lambda functions**: `\(x) (x, )` (also called anonymous functions) ## Summarize multiple columns with `across()` - To choose columns, you can use the same options as with `select()` . . . For each `type` of cereal, calculate the means of all numeric variables. . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="3"} cereal |> group_by(type) |> summarise(across(.cols = where(is.numeric), .fns = \(x) mean(x, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error: ! object 'cereal' not found ``` ::: ::: ## If you are struggling with `across()` :::incremental Break it down: 1. think about what the code would be for *one* column ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summarize(calories = mean(calories, na.rm = T)) ``` ::: 2. replace the column name with the placeholder `x` and add `\(x)` in front for the `.fns` argument. You have created a lambda function! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="2"} summarize(across(.cols = , .fns = \(x) mean(x, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: 3. think about **which** columns you want to apply this to for the `.cols` argument ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="1"} summarize(across(.cols = calories:potass, .fns = \(x) mean(x, na.rm = T))) ``` ::: ::: ## `across()`: Related Functions These functions are used with `filter()` to select rows based on a logical statement applied to multiple columns - `if_any()` -- returns a logical vector (one element for each row) that is `TRUE` if the logical statement is true for *any* column in the supplied columns - `if_all()` -- returns a logical vector (one element for each row) that is `TRUE` if the logical statement is true for *all* columns in the supplied columns ## `if_any()` Example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} colleges <- read_csv("https://www.dropbox.com/s/bt5hvctdevhbq6j/colleges.csv?dl=1") colleges_clean <- colleges |> select(INSTNM, CITY, STABBR, ZIP, ADM_RATE, SAT_AVG, UGDS, TUITIONFEE_IN, TUITIONFEE_OUT, CONTROL, REGION) |> filter(CONTROL == 1 | CONTROL == 2) ``` ::: Remember, you got warnings in PA3 when converting some columns to numeric? If you look at the original data, you can see this is because missing values were indicated with the string `"NULL"`. 🧩 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} colleges_clean |> slice_head(n = 3) |> knitr::kable() |> kableExtra::scroll_box(height = "175px") |> kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 20) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
INSTNM CITY STABBR ZIP ADM_RATE SAT_AVG UGDS TUITIONFEE_IN TUITIONFEE_OUT CONTROL REGION
Alabama A & M University Normal AL 35762 0.9027 929 4824 9857 18236 1 5
University of Alabama at Birmingham Birmingham AL 35294-0110 0.9181 1195 12866 8328 19032 1 5
Amridge University Montgomery AL 36117-3553 NULL NULL 322 6900 6900 2 5
````` ::: ::: . . . We could drop these rows before converting the columns to numeric if desired, using `if_any()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} colleges_clean <- colleges_clean |> filter(!if_any(.cols = ADM_RATE:TUITIONFEE_OUT, .fns = \(x) x == "NULL")) ``` ::: ## Using `kable()` for formatting in labs - When printing rows of a data frame or tibble - need to load the `knitr` package at the beginning of your file - `kable()` outputs a **markdown version** of your data - should only be used to nicely format data you are **printing** :::columns :::column **e.g.** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal |> mutate(ratio = sugars / potass) |> slice_head(n = 3) |> kable() ``` ::: ::: :::column **NOT** ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cereal_clean <- cereal |> mutate(ratio = sugars / potass) |> slice_head(n = 3) |> kable() ``` ::: ::: ::: ## To do... - **Lab 3: Teacher Evaluations** - Due Sunday, 4/19 at 11:59pm - **Required [Reading](../../reading/week-3.2.qmd)** - Review all material from this week for the **group quiz on Monday**! - Make sure that you can answer all of the questions in this week's notes! - Group quiz at the **beginning** of class - Reminder: rescheduled office hours 12-1:30 pm tomorrow
